summary: When inheriting a failed data project, the key is to resist the urge to start with technology and instead focus first on understanding stakeholder needs, uncovering 'dark matter workflows' (shadow IT solutions built outside the data stack), and investigating the political and organizational reasons the project failed. Success requires building relationships, setting realistic expectations, and making stakeholders the heroes rather than positioning the data team as saviors.
key_points: Start by understanding business stakeholder goals and decisions, not technology
Investigate 'dark matter workflows' - Excel-based Franken-stacks that power business units outside the official data stack
Treat the failed project like a crime scene to uncover political and organizational root causes
Ensure you have actual authority to make changes, not just accountability and responsibility
Technology assessment comes after understanding business context and failure reasons
Make stakeholders look like heroes and avoid making it about the data team's success
money_quotes: Like the dark matter that you can't see, but supposedly comprises most of the universe, dark matter workflows are things you don't see that power the business.
You might be granted a lot of accountability and responsibility but with very little authority.
Until you understand the business objectives and reasons why the data project failed, technology doesn't matter.
Organizations are dynamic and political. In my experience, data projects fail because data teams don't properly address stakeholder needs.
Shine a light on the business stakeholders and make them look like heroes. Make them part of the project's success since you serve them.
key_insight: Failed data projects are rarely just technical failures—they're organizational and political failures that require understanding stakeholder needs and building coalitions before touching the technology.
